title: Ratiometric analysis of the ferrocene boronate esters of 2- and 4-hydroxyestradiol by tandem electrospray mass spectrometry
abstract:
Ratiometric analysis of the ferrocene boronate esters of
2- and 4-hydroxyestradiol by tandem electrospray mass
spectrometry
Dudley Williams, Shiuan Chen and Mary K. Young*
City of Hope National Medical Center and Beckman Research Institute, Division of Immunology, 1450 E. Duarte Rd., Duarte, CA 91010,
USA
Received 30 October 2000; Revised 1 December 2000; Accepted 2 December 2000
A method has been developed for the semiquantitative analysis of the catechol estrogens, 2- and 4-
hydroxyestradiol, using tandem electrospray mass spectrometry in a quadrupole ion trap mass
analyzer. The implication of catechol estrogens in the biogenesis of breast and prostate cancer makes
these labile lipophilic compounds important analytical targets. Ferrocene boronic acid is reacted
with 2- and 4-hydroxyestradiol to form their cyclic boronate esters. Sample ionization is
accomplished during the electrospray process by a one-electron oxidation of the ferrocene
functionality to form the radical cation. The analysis depends on a non-aqueous solvent system
consisting of 90% acetonitrile and 10% dichloromethane with 100 mM lithium triflate as the
supporting electrolyte. The sensitivity of the analysis is greatly increased by the use of a novel
electrospray interface with a large surface area stainless steel electrode coupled to a pulled fused-
silica needle. Collision-induced dissociation of the selected molecular ion within the ion trap
produces fragment ion spectra that can be used to distinguish between the two isobaric isomers and
ultimately determine the relative amounts in mixtures containing both components. The method is
sensitive to analyte concentrations in the low nM range. Copyright # 2001 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
The catechol estrogens, 2-hydroxyestradiol (2-OHE) and 4-
hydroxyestradiol (4-OHE), are implicated as so-called good
and bad estrogens in the biogenesis of malignant cells.1±4 It
has been demonstrated that these compounds can be formed
by cytochrome P450 hydroxylation of estradiol.2 Modulation
of these products is controlled through the action of catechol-
O-methyl transferases, which methylate the newly intro-
duced phenolic hydroxyl group. The so-called good estrogen
(2-OHE) is thought to maintain positive roles within the cell
such as the suppression of osteoporosis and atherosclerosis
because of its ability to inhibit leucotriene synthesis5 (a
potent stimulator of bone re-absorption). On the other hand,
4-OHE is prone to easy oxidation. If it avoids methylation,
glucuronidation, sulfation or other neutralizing processes, 4-
OHE may become oxidized to the semiquinone. The
semiquinone is redox reactive and has been shown to
depurinate DNA and produce significantly increased num-
bers of mammary cancers in animal models.4,6 An analogous
mechanism has been postulated for the development of
prostate cancer.7 To better understand the links between
these two bioeffector molecules in the context of both breast
and prostate cancer, sensitive methods of analysis for these
compounds are essential. To date, the most common forms
of chemical analysis for catechol estrogens have been gas
chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) or radio-
metric (tritium release).8,9 These latter methods, although
sensitive, all possess certain drawbacks, such as the require-
ment for manipulation of radioactive materials or, in the case
of GC/MS, the risk of thermal degradation of fragile
analytes. Electrospray mass spectrometry (ES-MS)
has
proven to be a robust and sensitive method for the detection
and quantitation of both large and small molecules.10
However, most analyses involve aqueous/organic liquid
mixtures containing analytes that have easily ionizable
functional groups. The ES-MS analysis of neutral lipophilic
compounds such as 2- and 4-OHE requires the use of both a
suitably non-polar solvent and a methodology for creating a
charged species. Using the technology pioneered by Van
Berkel and co-workers,11 we have developed a sensitive and
semiquantitative method for analyzing these isobaric hydro-
xyestradiol isomers in the presence of one another. The
methodology utilizes ferrocenyl boronic acid derivatives12±15
that undergo single-electron oxidation when sprayed from
an electrospray interface with a large surface area elec-
trode.11,16±18 Fragment ion spectra from the molecular
radical cations of the two isomers are sufficiently different
that their relative ratios in a mixture can be determined.
EXPERIMENTAL
Chemicals
All
solvents,
dichloromethane
(DCM)
and
acetonitrile
*Correspondence to: M. K. Young, City of Hope Medical Center
and Beckman Research Institute, Division of Immunology, 1450
E. Duarte Rd., Duarte, CA 91010, USA.
Contract/grant sponsor: National Institutes of Health; Con-
tract/grant number: RR06217; Contract/grant number: ES08258.
Contract/grant sponsor: Cancer Center Core; Contract/grant
number: CA33752.
Copyright # 2001 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
RAPID COMMUNICATIONS IN MASS SPECTROMETRY
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 2001; 15: 182±186
 (MeCN) were supplied either by Burdick and Jackson
(Muskegon. WI, USA), or Aldrich Chemical Co., (Milwau-
kee, WI, USA). Ferrocene boronate and lithium triflate
(LiTrf) were also supplied by Aldrich, and the 2- and 4-
hydroxyestradiol (OHE) analyte standards (Fig. 1(A),(B))
were obtained from Sigma Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO,
USA).
Mass spectrometry
All spectra were obtained using a Finnigan LCQ quadrupole
ion trap mass spectrometer (ThermoQuest Corp., San Jose,
CA, USA). The factory-supplied electrospray head was
replaced with a previously described microspray interface
mounted on an xyz-positioner.19 The high-voltage electrode
was constructed from concentric stainless steel tubes 350 mm
o.d./150 mm i.d. and 100 mm o.d./50 mm i.d., previously
described by us for the analysis of carbohydrates.20 This
configuration behaves as an electrochemical cell suitable for
the pre-formation of ions by oxidation of ferrocene.
Tandem MS fragment ion spectra, derived from the
molecular ions of the OHE complexes observed at m/z 482,
were obtained at 44% relative collision energy. The correla-
tion of the fragment ion spectra to the relative ratios of the
two isomers in sample mixtures was established by plotting
the % ratio of the ion at m/z 454 to the base peak at m/z 438 as
a function of the isomer mole ratio.
Sample preparation
The 2- and 4-OHE standards were prepared by dissolving
measured quantities
(1±4 mg) of the
two analytes
in
dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). The stock solutions were
further diluted to the desired concentration using 80%
MeCN/10% water/10% DCM. A 10 mL aliquot of a LiTrf
stock solution was added to 1 mL of each of the working
solutions to achieve a final LiTrf concentration of approxi-
mately 100 mM. The stock solution of LiTrf was prepared by
dissolving 2 mg of LiTrf in 1 mL of MeCN.
The 2- and 4-OHE stock solutions may be stored frozen at
ÿ15°C for up to 3 months. The 4-OHE/DMSO solution,
however, quickly starts to decompose if left at room
temperature, as evidenced by an increasing yellow colora-
tion. Sample solutions were kept frozen until just prior to
use. The working solution containing the diluted analytes
and LiTrf electrolyte was transferred to a 250 mL syringe,
coupled via a short fused-silica capillary transfer line to the
electrospray interface. The solutions were infused at an
initial rate of 3 mL/min during which time the instrument
was tuned for signal strength by adjusting the spray
position, spray voltage and flow rate. A voltage of 1±1.5 kV
usually produces an adequate signal. The signal strength is
flow-rate dependent and the optimal flow rate in our system
was 500 nL/min.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Both 2- and 4-OHE exhibit a vicinal diol functionality that
may be exploited by converting these electrospray neutral
molecules into ferrocene boronate complexes (Fig. 1) that are
readily amenable to analysis by tandem ES-MS. Previously,
cyclic boronate esters have been utilized as protecting
groups,21 and the ferrocene boronates have been used to
prepare stable products suitable for analysis by gas chroma-
tography/mass
spectrometry
(GC/MS).12,13,22
Ferrocene
boronate has a low redox potential (0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl14,23)
which allows it to be readily oxidized in preference to other
organic species that generally have higher redox potentials
(aprotic solvents). A supporting electrolyte is required in
such solvents in order to provide sufficient current for the
Figure 1. Structures of catechol estrogens and their ferrocenyl boronic ester complexes. (A) 4-
Hydroxyestradiol: mass 288 u. (B) Ferrocenyl boronic ester of 4-Hydroxyestradiol: mass 482 u. (C) 2-
Hydroxyestradiol. (D) Ferrocenyl boronic ester of 2-Hydroxyestradiol. Please note: mass units = u.
Copyright # 2001 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 2001; 15: 182±186
ES-MS/MS of ferrocene boronate esters of 2- and 4-hydroxyestradiols

ES-MS/MS of ferrocene boronate esters of 2- and 4-hydroxyestradiols
electrochemical reaction. Van Berkel and co-workers have
shown that LiTrf functions well as a supporting electrolyte
while keeping background ions at minimal levels.16 Any
electrospray source is intrinsically an electrochemical cell,
and the one-electron oxidation of cyclic ferrocenyl boronate
esters of suitable diols is readily observed using standard
source designs. However, in these studies, signal strength
was found to increase by as much as two orders of
magnitude by increasing the surface area of the high-voltage
electrode in contact with the sample solution. This was
accomplished using concentric stainless steel tubes coupled
to a pulled fused-silica electrospray needle as shown in our
previous work.20 The signal strength of the analyte ions was
flow-rate dependent and the small dimensions (5±10 mm) of
Figure 2. Full scan ES-MS of 2- or 4-hydroxyestradiol ferrocenyl boronate complex showing a radical
cation at 482 u (calculated mass, 482.47 u). The ions for the derivatizing agent and its monodehydrated
form are shown at 230 and 212 u. Unspecified ions shown are derived from solvent cluster/derivatizing
Figure 3. MS2 fragmentation spectra of 482 u selected ion (A) 2-Hydroxyestradiol ferrocene
boronate and (B) 4-hydroxyestradiol ferrocene boronate. The first three high-mass fragment ions
suggest different fragmentation pathways for the two isomeric ions. Both spectra are normalized to
the 438 u ion, providing a ratiometric difference to the neutral loss derived fragment observed at
454 u. The m/z 454 is produced by the elimination of ethylene (ÿ28 u). The residual ion at m/z 212
arises from the loss of water from the derivatizing agent. This is also seen in the full scan mass

Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 2001; 15: 182±186
D. Williams, S. Chen and M. K. Young
the electrospray needle aperture provide stable operation
over a broad range of flow rates without the use of sheath
The ferrocene boronic acid derivatizing agent produces a
suite of ions with the principal peak of the cluster appearing
at m/z 230 (Fig. 2). The natural abundance of iron and boron
isotopes creates a complex pattern of ions for all species
containing these elements. Infusion of either 2-OHE or 4-
OHE ferrocene boronate esters produces a peak at m/z 482
corresponding to the molecular ion. Selection and collision-
induced dissociation (CID) of that ion in the trap produces a
rich fragment ion spectrum (Fig. 3). The CID spectra for the
two isomers are very similar with a base peak at m/z 438.
However, the fragment at m/z 454, which is likely derived
from the neutral loss of ethylene from the `C' ring, is much
more intense for the 4-OHE isomer (74 vs. 17%). Spraying
mixtures of the two isomer standards yields data for a plot of
the mole ratio of the isomers as a function of the intensity of
the m/z 454 ion measured in turn as a percentage of base peak
(m/z 438) (Fig. 4). In this way, the signal strength of the ion at
m/z 454 may be used to estimate the isomeric composition of
any mixture. The limits of detection for this method
employing the single-electron ferrocenyl boronate esters
are the nM level. This is a requirement for future biologically
We hope to use this protocol and the techniques discussed
above in the estimation of the content of these two important
catechol estrogens, 2-OHE and 4-OHE, in biological samples.
Circulating levels of the catechol estrogens have been
estimated to be in the low nM concentration level. Further
evolution of the method described above will focus on the
development of a pre-concentration cleanup step and low-
resolution liquid chromatography (LC) separation. In this
manner, without depending on rigorous chromatography to
provide a possibly difficult separation of two isomers, an
accurate ratio of these two hormones may be obtained.
Additionally, we are applying these techniques to the other
related estrone metabolites that have also been implicated in
redox cycling mechanisms displayed by breast tumor cells.
